templates.title: Pi-Hole
templates.description: An application to block ads and internet trackers, for all devices on your home network.
templates.name: pi-hole

env.name: FTLCONF_webserver_api_password
env.label: Web Server Password
env.description: Password for the admin web server. Will be set to a random value if not provided.

env.name: FTLCONF_webserver_port
env.label: Web Server Port(s)
env.description: Ports to be used by the admin web server.

env.name: FTLCONF_dns_port
env.label: DNS port
env.description: Ports to be used by the DNS server.

templates.note:
Application Data

The container is pre-configured to store persistent data for Pi-Hole under /srv/appdata/pihole. Persistent storage is required to remember any configuration change made through the admin website.

You may change the location by expanding Show advanced options below, then look under Volume mapping.

To create the application data folder:

mkdir -p /srv/appdata
chown $USER /srv/appdata

Timezone

It is recommended to set the timezone variable according to your geographic location. See this wiki page for possible values.

Network Configuration

⬩ Recommended mode: host

It is recommended to set the network of this container to host. By default, pi-hole will use port 53 to listen for DNS requests, and ports 80/443 for the admin website. If you want to use different ports when running in host network mode, change the respective environment variables below.

Web Server Port(s) Format

The value should be a comma-separated list of ports to listen on. It is possible to specify an IP address to bind to. In this case, an IP address and a colon must be prepended to the port number. For example, to bind to the loopback interface on port 80 (IPv4) and to all interfaces port 8080 (IPv4), use 127.0.0.1:80,8080. [::]:80 can be used to listen to IPv6 connections to port 80. IPv6 addresses of network interfaces can be specified as well, e.g. [::1]:80 for the IPv6 loopback interface. [::]:80 will bind to port 80 IPv6 only.

In order to use port 80 for all interfaces, both IPv4 and IPv6, use either the configuration 80,[::]:80 (create one socket for IPv4 and one for IPv6 only), or +80 (create one socket for both, IPv4 and IPv6). The '+' notation to use IPv4 and IPv6 will only work if no network interface is specified. Depending on your operating system version and IPv6 network environment, some configurations might not work as expected, so you have to test to find the configuration most suitable for your needs. In case +80 does not work for your environment, you need to use 80,[::]:80.
If the port is TLS/SSL, a letter 's' (secure) must be appended, for example, 80,443s will open port 80 and port 443, and connections on port 443 will be encrypted. For non-encrypted ports, it is allowed to append letter 'r' (as in redirect). Redirected ports will redirect all their traffic to the first configured SSL port. For example, if webserver.port is 80r,443s, then all HTTP traffic coming at port 80 will be redirected to HTTPS port 443.

When specifying 'o' (optional) behind a port, inability to use this port is not considered an error. For instance, specifying 80o,8080o will allow the webserver to listen on either 80, 8080, both or even none of the two ports. This flag may be combined with 'r' and 's' like 80or,443os,8080,4443s (80 redirecting to SSL if available, 443 encrypted if available, 8080 mandatory and unencrypted, 4443 mandatory and encrypted).

If this value is not set (empty string), the web server will not be started and, hence, the API will not be available.

⬩ Alternative mode: bridge

It is possible to run pi-hole in bridge network mode instead. The downside is that all connected clients may appears to have the IP of the bridge network gateway (e.g. 172.17.0.1). This does not affect the ad-blocking functionality of the DNS server, but the admin website dashboards may not be as useful for investigation purposes.

When running in bridge network mode, make sure to add mappings for the following container ports:

+----------------+--------------------+----------+
| Container Port | Purpose            | Optional |
+----------------+--------------------+----------+
| 53/tcp, 53/udp | DNS ports          | no       |
| 80/tcp         | Admin site (HTTP)  | yes      |
| 443/tcp        | Admin site (HTTPS) | yes      |
+----------------+--------------------+----------+

templates.title: Linux Server Monitoring [arm|arm64]
templates.description: A pre-configured Linux monitoring solution using Docker.
templates.name: linux-monitoring

env.name: APPDATA_LOCATION
env.label: App Data Location
env.description: Directory used by containers in the stack for persistent storage

env.name: PROMETHEUS_RETENTION_TIME
env.label: Prometheus Data Retention Time
env.description: How long to store metrics data in Prometheus

env.name: PROMETHEUS_RETENTION_SIZE
env.label: Prometheus Data Retention Size
env.description: Largest size to allow Prometheus data to grow before trimming

env.name: PROMETHEUS_ENABLE_REMOTE_WRITES
env.label: Enable Remote Writes for Prometheus
env.description: Enable the Prometheus API endpoint that receives remote write requests

env.name: PROMETHEUS_SERVER_PORT
env.label: Prometheus Server Port
env.description: Port where the Prometheus web portal and API endpoint will be accessible

env.name: GRAFANA_WEB_PORT
env.label: Grafana Web Port
env.description: Port where the Grafana website will be accessible

templates.note:
Application data MUST be set up BEFORE deploying this stack by running the following on the server being deployed: 

wget -qO- https://thedebuggedlife.github.io/portainer-templates/appdata/linux-monitoring.sh | bash

See the template README or the related blog post for more info.

templates.title: Tailscale
templates.description: A secure, peer-to-peer VPN based on WireGuard that lets you create a private network between your devices using encrypted tunnels and automatic NAT traversal.
templates.name: tailscale

env.name: TS_AUTH_ONCE
env.label: Remember Auth Token
env.description: Attempt to log in only if not already logged in. False by default, to forcibly log in every time the container starts.

env.name: TS_AUTHKEY
env.label: Auth Key
env.description: An auth key used to authenticate the container.

env.name: TS_STATE_DIR
env.label: Tailscaled State Location
env.description: Directory where the state of tailscaled is stored. This needs to persist across container restarts.

env.name: TS_ENABLE_METRICS
env.label: Enable Metrics Endpoint
env.description: Set to true to enable an unauthenticated /metrics endpoint at the Local Address and Port (TS_LOCAL_ADDR_PORT).

templates.note:
Application Data

The container is pre-configured to store persistent data for Tailscale under /srv/appdata/tailscale. You may change the location by expanding Show advanced options below, then look under Volume mapping.

To create the application data folder:

mkdir -p /srv/appdata
chown $USER /srv/appdata

Timezone

It is recommended to set the timezone variable according to your geographic location. See this wiki page for possible values.

Network Configuration

⬩ Recommended mode: host

It is recommended to set the network of this container to host. This gives other authorized clients connected to the Tailscale network access to all services running on the host.

⬩ Alternative mode: bridge

It is possible to run Tailscale in bridge network mode instead. If you run on this mode, you may need to:

→ Add the environment variable TS_DEST_IP and set it to the IP address of the gateway for the bridge network, e.g. 172.17.0.1
→ Add the environment variable TS_HOSTNAME and set it to the machine name of the container host.
→ Add a mapping for the port specified in the environment variable TS_LOCAL_ADDR_PORT (defaults to 9002) if the health-check or metrics endpoint is enabled

templates.description: Collects server metrics and sends them to a central Prometheus aggregation service.
